content.text: IC Training and Simulation Exercises

Reading an incident runbook produces passive knowledge. Commanding an incident produces decision-making muscle memory. The gap between the two is exactly the gap that allows an incident to spin out of control under a first-time IC. The training program closes that gap through a deliberately staged sequence of learning experiences that escalate in fidelity and consequence until the IC candidate has commanded real scenarios under real pressure with a safety net.
Training Path Overview

attrs.alt: IC certification training path from fundamentals course to certified IC

content.text: Phase 1: Fundamentals Course

The async fundamentals course establishes shared vocabulary and mental models across the IC cohort. Its goal is not to teach incident management—that requires live experience—but to ensure every IC candidate enters the tabletop simulation with the same baseline knowledge.

Module 1 (45 min): IC role definition, decision authority, and what the IC explicitly does not do. Includes case studies of incidents that went poorly when the IC started debugging instead of coordinating.
Module 2 (45 min): Incident taxonomy and classification decision tree. The IC must be able to classify an incident within the first 2 minutes of a page.
Module 3 (60 min): Communication protocols—bridge call cadence, status page update format, stakeholder notification timing, and when to involve Legal and CISO.
Module 4 (45 min): Incident closure and handoff—the 30-minute stable-metrics rule, postmortem assignment, handoff document format, and all-clear declaration.
Assessment: 20-question quiz with 85% pass threshold. Failed assessments require a 72-hour wait and review of the relevant module before re-attempt.
Phase 2: Tabletop Simulation

The tabletop simulation is a live 3-hour exercise run by a certified senior IC trainer. It replicates the pressure, ambiguity, and rapid information flow of a real incident without real consequences. The trainer injects complications at timed intervals to test how the candidate responds to new information that changes the incident's classification, severity, or required notifications.

# Tabletop Simulation Script — Safety Escalation Scenario

## Setup (read to candidate before starting timer)
You are the primary IC. It is 14:32 UTC on a Tuesday.
You receive a PagerDuty alert: user_harm_report from the support queue.
The report reads: "Your AI assistant told me to mix bleach and ammonia to clean
my kitchen. I want to know who is responsible."

Your current on-call team:
- Technical Lead: Priya Sharma (AI Platform)
- Scribe: Marcus Lee
- Trust & Safety on-call: Carol James (just started a meeting, will respond in 5 min)

## T+0: Initial State
Instructions to candidate: "The page just arrived. What do you do first?"
Expected actions: Open incident channel, page T&S, assign severity, start scribe
Red flags: Starts debugging before opening incident channel; fails to page T&S immediately

## T+3 min: Injection 1
Trainer: "A second user report arrives with a screenshot of the same output.
          The screenshot is now being shared on Twitter with your company mentioned."
Expected actions: Escalate to SEV-1; notify Legal; trigger exec notification
Red flags: Doesn't escalate severity; continues treating as SEV-3

## T+8 min: Injection 2
Trainer: "Carol from T&S is on the bridge. She says the safety classifier logs show
          the output was blocked, but somehow the raw response was returned to the user.
          This looks like a bug in the response filtering layer."
Expected actions: Redirect Technical Lead to response filtering; consider suspending endpoint
Red flags: Stays on root cause discovery instead of deciding on suspension

## T+15 min: Injection 3
Trainer: "Engineering VP joins the bridge and asks: 'Should we suspend the endpoint?
          What's the blast radius?'"
Expected actions: IC makes a clear recommendation based on available information
Red flags: Defers to VP instead of providing IC recommendation; unable to estimate blast radius

## T+25 min: Wrap-up
Trainer stops the scenario. Structured debrief follows:
- What went well?
- What would you do differently?
- Were all required notifications sent on time?
- Was the severity escalation timely?

## Passing Criteria
- T&S paged within 2 min of starting
- Severity escalated within 4 min of second user report
- Legal notified within 5 min of escalation to SEV-1
- Clear recommendation made to VP at T+15
- Incident channel opened and scribe assigned within 2 min
Phase 3: Shadow Shift

During the shadow shift, the candidate joins the primary IC on their on-call week as an observer. The candidate attends every incident bridge call, reviews every status page draft before it goes out, and participates in post-incident debriefs. Crucially, the candidate shadows regardless of whether incidents occur—they also participate in proactive drills, on-call handoffs, and roster maintenance tasks.
Week start: candidate reviews the IC handoff document from the outgoing IC, verifies the on-call roster is staffed, and confirms runbook links are current.
During any incident: candidate attends bridge calls, follows the primary IC's decisions in real time, and writes their own parallel incident log to compare with the official scribe record afterward.
Post-incident: candidate participates in the debrief and writes a 1-page shadow reflection covering what they would have done differently and any gaps in their knowledge revealed by the incident.
Week end: candidate submits the shadow reflection to the IC trainer for review. Trainer provides written feedback within 5 business days.
Phase 4: Solo Shift Observed

The observed solo shift is the highest-fidelity training experience. The candidate commands all incidents for a full week as the primary IC. A certified IC mentor is designated on standby—they do not participate unless the candidate explicitly requests help or is about to make a decision with significant irreversible consequences.

The mentor's non-intervention rule is important. An IC trainer who jumps in every time the candidate looks uncertain produces ICs who freeze and wait for rescue under real pressure. The mentor intervenes only when: the candidate requests it; the candidate is about to take an action that would cause greater harm than the incident itself (e.g., deleting production data); or the candidate has been silent on the bridge for more than 3 minutes with no visible plan.
Quarterly Drill Schedule

Certification without ongoing practice degrades. Certified ICs who have not commanded an incident in the past 60 days need a refresher before their next solo on-call week. The quarterly drill provides that refresher through a condensed 90-minute tabletop that rotates scenario categories each quarter.

# quarterly-drill-schedule.yaml
drills:
  - quarter: Q1
    scenario_category: infrastructure
    focus: rate_limit_cascade_with_backup_failure
    duration_minutes: 90
    required_participants:
      - all_primary_ics
      - sre_on_call_leads
    optional:
      - engineering_managers

  - quarter: Q2
    scenario_category: safety
    focus: multi_user_harm_with_media_pressure
    duration_minutes: 90
    required_participants:
      - all_primary_ics
      - trust_and_safety_leads
      - legal_representative

  - quarter: Q3
    scenario_category: security
    focus: prompt_injection_data_exfil_scenario
    duration_minutes: 90
    required_participants:
      - all_primary_ics
      - security_team_leads
      - ciso_or_delegate

  - quarter: Q4
    scenario_category: capability
    focus: silent_quality_degradation_with_user_impact
    duration_minutes: 90
    required_participants:
      - all_primary_ics
      - ai_platform_leads

assessment_criteria:
  passing_threshold: 80  # percent of expected actions taken on time
  remediation_required_below: 70
  remediation: repeat_tabletop_with_trainer_within_30_days
  documentation: training_records/ic-drills/
Simulation Debrief Framework

The debrief is where learning is consolidated. A simulation without a structured debrief produces much less learning improvement than one with disciplined reflection. The OODA (Observe, Orient, Decide, Act) loop debrief framework works well for incident simulations because it mirrors the cognitive process the IC follows during the actual incident.
OODA Phase
Debrief Question
Common Gap
Observe
What signals did you notice first? What did you miss?
Missed user report while focusing on metrics
Orient
How did your mental model of the incident change as new information arrived?
Anchored to initial classification too long
Decide
Which decisions took longest? What information were you waiting for?
Sought consensus instead of making call
Act
Were your communications clear and timely? What would you say differently?
Status page update delayed past 30-min cadence
Tracking Training Records
Key Takeaways
Training stages must be sequential and cannot be skipped. Each stage builds on the previous; an IC who completes the fundamentals course and jumps to solo on-call is not trained—they are hazardous.
Tabletop simulations require injected complications to be valuable. A scenario that unfolds without surprises does not prepare the IC for the information uncertainty of a real incident.
The solo-shift mentor must enforce non-intervention by default. Intervening too quickly produces ICs who freeze and wait for rescue rather than commanding under pressure.
Quarterly drills maintain perishable skills. An IC who certified 11 months ago and has not commanded an incident since needs a refresher before their next primary rotation.
Track training records in version-controlled YAML, not spreadsheets. The IC roster health checker should read from this file to automatically flag lapsed certifications.
